title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a marine archaeologist called Julian Vickers giving a talk about his work on a shipwreck discovery in the Mediterranean. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a marine archaeologist called Julian Vickers giving a talk about his work on a shipwreck discovery in the Mediterranean. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Julian Vickers describes the discovery and excavation of an ancient Roman cargo ship.
extracts.audio_script:
Good morning. My name is Julian Vickers, and I’m a marine archaeologist. Today, I want to share the story of a project that has occupied my team for the last three years: the excavation of the 'Neptune’s Wealth', a Roman merchant vessel found off the coast of Sicily. Most people assume we find these sites using high-tech satellites, but this particular story began quite differently. It was actually a [7]local fisherman[/7] who first alerted us to the site. He had been complaining for years about his nets getting snagged on something unusual, and when he finally pulled up a fragment of a ceramic jar, he knew he’d found something significant. 

When we first dived down to the wreck, we were amazed by its condition. The ship was lying at a depth of forty meters, which is just at the limit for conventional scuba equipment. What was immediately apparent was that the [8]sandy seabed[/8] had acted as a perfect preservative. Over two millennia, the currents had buried the lower half of the hull, shielding the organic timbers from oxygen and wood-boring organisms. It’s rare to find wood that is still structural after two thousand years, but here it was, almost looking as if it had sunk yesterday.

The cargo was equally fascinating. We found hundreds of amphorae—large clay jars used for transport. While many of them were empty, we were thrilled to discover that several still contained traces of [9]pickled fish[/9], a delicacy exported across the Roman Empire. Analyzing these remains gives us an incredible window into the diet and economy of the period. It’s like opening a pantry door that has been locked since the first century.

Working underwater presents unique challenges that you just don't face on a land-based dig. The most significant obstacle for our divers was the [10]variable visibility[/10] caused by afternoon currents. On a good day, you could see for fifteen meters; on a bad day, you couldn't see your own hand in front of your mask. We had to develop a system of tactile markers to ensure no one got disoriented while moving between the wreck and the surface lines.

To document the site accurately, we used a process called photogrammetry. This involved taking thousands of high-resolution photos and stitching them together. I was particularly impressed by the [11]3D model[/11] we were able to create as a result. It allowed us to study the ship’s construction in a virtual environment without having to disturb the fragile remains unnecessarily. This digital twin of the wreck is now being used by researchers all over the world.

During the second year of the dig, I had my most memorable moment. I was clearing away some silt near what we believed was the galley, or the ship's kitchen, when I spotted a glint of metal. It turned out to be a [12]silver coin[/12] minted during the reign of Emperor Claudius. Finding currency is vital because it provides us with a precise date for the shipwreck, allowing us to place the vessel within a specific historical context. 

People often ask me about the physical toll of this job. Beyond the fitness required for deep diving, you need [13]excellent concentration[/13] to perform delicate tasks while managing your air supply and decompression limits. You are constantly multitasking in a high-pressure environment. It’s exhausting, but the thrill of discovery keeps you going.

As we move into the final phase of the project, our focus has shifted to the public. We are currently designing a [14]touring exhibition[/14] that will showcase the most significant finds from the wreck. We want people to experience the mystery of the deep without having to put on a wetsuit. It’s been a privilege to bring this piece of history back to the surface, and I hope it inspires the next generation of explorers. Thank you.

questions.question: Julian explains that the shipwreck was originally located by a [7]___[/7] working in the area.
questions.explanation: He mentions it was 'actually a local fisherman who first alerted us to the site'.

questions.question: Julian says that the [8]___[/8] helped to keep the ship’s timber in good condition.
questions.explanation: He states that the 'sandy seabed had acted as a perfect preservative'.

questions.question: Researchers found evidence that the ship had been carrying [9]___[/9] inside some of the jars.
questions.explanation: Julian says several jars 'contained traces of pickled fish'.

questions.question: The main difficulty for the divers was the [10]___[/10] they encountered in the water.
questions.explanation: He identifies 'variable visibility caused by afternoon currents' as the most significant obstacle.

questions.question: Julian was very pleased with the [11]___[/11] that was produced using digital photography.
questions.explanation: He says he was 'particularly impressed by the 3D model we were able to create'.

questions.question: The team was able to date the wreck accurately after finding a [12]___[/12] on the site.
questions.explanation: The discovery of a 'silver coin minted during the reign of Emperor Claudius' provided the date.

questions.question: Julian believes that having [13]___[/13] is essential for anyone doing his type of work.
questions.explanation: He notes that 'you need excellent concentration to perform delicate tasks'.

questions.question: Julian is currently helping to organize a [14]___[/14] to share the project with the public.
questions.explanation: He mentions they are 'currently designing a touring exhibition'.
